common_name: Wonderful Pomegranate (Classic Large Deep-Red Gulf South Pomegranate)

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root trees in late winter to spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral to slightly alkaline (pH 5.5–7.5) soil in full sun; Wonderful is highly drought-tolerant once established — it is one of the most xeric fruit trees available and performs well on minimal irrigation in well-drained Gulf South soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun; Wonderful is the dominant commercial pomegranate variety of California and the standard against which all other pomegranate varieties are evaluated — producing very large (10–16 oz), round, deep-red to scarlet-red fruits with a thin, hard rind enclosing hundreds of ruby-red arils (the juicy, seed-containing sacs) with a complex tart-sweet, deeply flavored, astringent juice that defines the classic pomegranate flavor profile. The ornamental quality of pomegranate is exceptional — showy orange-red flowers (May–July in zone 9), then large, jewel-colored fruits that ripen to deep red-purple by October–November. Pomegranate is an ancient cultivated fruit (documented cultivation for 4,000+ years) that is one of the most drought-tolerant, heat-adapted, long-lived fruit trees available for Gulf South gardens. In zone 9, pomegranate thrives in the hot summers and mild winters — requiring only 150–200 chill hours — and established trees are remarkably long-lived, with historic specimens surviving 50–100+ years with minimal care. The ornamental summer flowers and autumn fruit make pomegranate as valuable as a landscape specimen tree as it is as a fruit producer. Self-fruitful (does not require a pollinator) and resistant to most pests and diseases common in Gulf South gardens. Harvest fruit when the exterior skin has fully colored and the fruit sounds hollow when tapped.

common_name: Angel Red Pomegranate (Soft-Seeded Sweet Red Fresh-Eating Pomegranate)

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root trees in late winter to spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral to slightly alkaline (pH 5.5–7.5) soil in full sun; Angel Red's soft seeds make fresh eating far more practical than hard-seeded varieties — the entire aril including the seed can be eaten without the tough, crunchy seed of standard pomegranates.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun; Angel Red is a soft-seeded pomegranate variety — one of the most important distinctions in pomegranate selection for fresh eating. Standard pomegranate varieties (including Wonderful) contain hard, woody seeds at the center of each aril that some people find unpleasant to eat; soft-seeded varieties like Angel Red have soft, barely-noticeable seeds that allow the entire aril to be eaten without extraction, fundamentally changing the fresh-eating experience. Angel Red produces large, bright-red fruits with deep-red, juicy arils and rich pomegranate flavor — somewhat sweeter and less astringent than Wonderful at peak ripeness. The soft-seeded quality makes Angel Red excellent for fresh eating out of hand (where hard seeds are an obstacle), salads (where whole arils are used as a garnish), and for fresh juice where the softer seeds blend or press more easily. Like all pomegranates, Angel Red is self-fruitful, drought-tolerant, heat-adapted, and ornamentally attractive with showy orange-red flowers in summer and richly colored fall fruit. In zone 9, Angel Red ripens in October–November at essentially the same time as Wonderful — the harvest timing is similar but the eating experience is distinctly more accessible for fresh consumption.

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root plants in late fall to spring in well-drained, very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in full sun; Powderblue rabbit-eye blueberries require cross-pollination from another rabbit-eye variety (Brightwell, Tifblue, Climax) for adequate fruit set — plant at least two different rabbit-eye varieties within 50 feet.

directions: Plant in well-drained, very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in full sun; Powderblue is one of the most widely planted and highly regarded rabbit-eye blueberry varieties — producing large, light-blue to sky-blue, firm berries with excellent flavor and good fresh-eating and processing quality on a vigorously productive, heat-adapted bush that is among the most reliable blueberry types for zone 9 conditions. Rabbit-eye blueberries (Vaccinium virgatum, also classified as V. ashei) are the blueberry type most naturally adapted to the Gulf South — native to the coastal plain and Piedmont of the southeastern US, rabbit-eye varieties tolerate heat, drought, clay soils, and the region's soil conditions better than northern highbush types and are generally more productive and longer-lived in zone 9 landscapes. Powderblue is mid-season to mid-late season (June in zone 9), ripening after the early southern highbush varieties (Emerald, Jewel) and extending the blueberry harvest season. The vigorous, upright to spreading bushes reach 6–10 feet at maturity — larger than southern highbush varieties but very productive and long-lived, with established Powderblue bushes commonly producing for 30–50+ years. As with all blueberries, very acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) is essential — rabbit-eye varieties may tolerate slightly higher pH than southern highbush but still require highly acidic conditions for nutrient availability and healthy growth.

common_name: Hillbilly Tomato (Appalachian Bicolor Yellow-Orange Striped Heirloom Beefsteak)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Hillbilly is an Appalachian heirloom from West Virginia that has been preserved and distributed through regional seed-saving networks for decades before entering the commercial heirloom seed trade.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Hillbilly (also called Hillbilly Potato Leaf) is a West Virginia heirloom beefsteak tomato of Appalachian origin — a large (12–24 oz), irregularly shaped, golden-yellow-orange fruit streaked and mottled with red to produce the characteristic bicolor pattern of this historic mountain heirloom. The Hillbilly's distinctive coloration differs from closely related varieties (Pineapple, Big Rainbow) in its specific yellow-orange background tone and the characteristic way the red streaking distributes across the fruit surface in bold, irregular patches. The flavor is outstanding: very sweet, fruity, and low-acid with a mild, rich character that has made Hillbilly one of the most enthusiastically recommended heirlooms by those who have grown it. 'Hillbilly Potato Leaf' plants display the characteristic potato-leaf (undivided, smooth-margined) foliage common among certain old Appalachian heirloom lineages. As an open-pollinated heirloom, seeds save true to type season after season. In zone 9, Hillbilly produces best in the spring window (February–June) before summer heat arrives — the very large fruits require the full spring season to reach their finest size and flavor quality. Sturdy support is required for the vigorous indeterminate vines.

common_name: Mr. Stripey Tomato (Large Bicolor Yellow-Red Striped Heirloom Beefsteak)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Mr. Stripey is not the same as the British 'Tigerella' tomato — Tigerella is a smaller red-and-yellow striped variety; Mr. Stripey is a larger American heirloom bicolor with different size and flavor characteristics.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Mr. Stripey is a large bicolor heirloom beefsteak tomato producing very large (10–20 oz), somewhat flattened, irregularly shaped fruits with a yellow-to-golden exterior prominently striped and mottled with red or pink-red in an irregular, abstract pattern across the fruit surface. The bicolor exterior reveals a matching interior when sliced — yellow and red-tinted flesh arranged in an irregular pattern that makes each fruit slightly different in cross-section. The flavor is sweet and mild with low acidity — characteristic of yellow-dominant bicolor tomatoes — and with a melon-like, fruity quality that is outstanding in fresh eating. Mr. Stripey is an indeterminate heirloom with vigorous vines requiring sturdy support throughout the season. The very large size of the fruits makes them slow to mature — zone 9 gardeners should start seeds early (8 weeks before last frost) and plant out as early as safely possible to maximize the spring growing window before summer heat arrives. As with most large bicolor heirlooms, the thin skin requires careful handling. Not to be confused with the British variety 'Tigerella' which shares a similar name but is a much smaller (4–6 oz), earlier-maturing, red-with-yellow-striped variety of completely different character.
